channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Oral Lesions, Uptake Data, and Regulation: The Missing Conversation on Nicotine Pouches
item.description: Public health authorities are reevaluating nicotine regulation as smoking rates decline worldwide. The focus shifts towards safer oral nicotine pouches as an alternative to vapes. The debate emphasizes managing risks proportionately and avoiding past mistakes. US regulators approved nicotine pouches under a harm reduction context. Studies show pouch users are mainly trying to quit smoking. Concerns about youth uptake and oral health effects persist. Transparent risk communication is crucial. Innovations in product design aim to reduce oral irritation. Regulators face challenges in balancing access for adult smokers and preventing youth uptake. Nicotine pouches are recognized as a harm reduction tool compared to cigarettes, highlighting the need for evidence-based regulation promoting both protection and progress. (Published on July 23, 2026byvapingpost.com)

item.title: The new merchants of doubt
item.description: Doubt can be manufactured in more than one direction. In nicotine policy, selective evidence and repeated uncertainty can make lower-risk products appear more dangerous than they are, while the risks of continued smoking fade into the background. That matters because public understanding shapes regulation, and regulation shapes whether smokers see switching as credible. Public health should challenge misinformation without becoming a source of it. (Published on July 23, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: Is Bioceramic the Future of Nicotine Delivery? | #GFN26ScienceLab
item.description: A material usually associated with medical and technical applications is now entering the nicotine conversation. Bioceramic delivery raises questions about whether product design can change exposure, user experience and the risk profile of nicotine use. That makes it worth studying carefully rather than dismissing as just another nicotine product. Innovation will not replace evidence, but it may expand what harm reduction can offer adults who continue to use nicotine. (Published on July 23, 2026bygfn.tv)
